25th Reunion

Our 25th Reunion is scheduled for 13–14 November 2026. Taylor Mawyer is leading reunion planning, with special thanks to Ethan Weber for assistance with hotels. Updates will be posted as the date gets closer.

Class Giving

$1,200,100.01

Our class giving goal is $1,200,100.01, and we are currently at $485,000. The class has an established memorial scholarship honoring our brother rats. Donations may also support other funds or scholarships.

Open Donation Form

Other Ways to Pledge

There are several ways to support the VMI Foundation or the VMI Keydet Club through an estate plan or other planned gift.

Bequests in a Will or TrustLeave a specific dollar amount, personal property, real estate, or a percentage of your estate to VMI.
Beneficiary DesignationsName VMI as a primary or contingent beneficiary on an IRA, 401(k), 403(b), bank account, investment account, or life insurance policy.
Charitable Remainder Trusts or “Give It Twice” TrustsEstablish a trust that provides income to a spouse or children for life or for a set period, with the remaining balance later transferred to VMI.
Charitable Gift AnnuitiesMake a gift in exchange for fixed lifetime payments for one or two people, with the remaining funds later supporting VMI.
Donor Advised FundsContribute to a donor advised fund, receive an immediate tax deduction, and recommend future gifts to VMI.
Charitable EndowmentsCreate an endowed fund through a will or trust. The principal is invested, and annual income supports a chosen area of VMI.

Planned gifts may qualify donors for membership in the Legacy Society, which includes invitations to special events, recognition in publications, and a Legacy Pin.

Featured Brother Rats

Meet members of the VMI Class of 2001 and learn where life has taken them since graduation.

Matt Meyers

CEO, B.E. Meyers & Co.

What are you doing now? CEO of B.E. Meyers & Co. in Redmond, Washington, a defense technology manufacturer specializing in combat-proven laser systems for U.S. Special Operations Command and other services within the U.S. Department of War, as well as law enforcement and international defense and aerospace customers. “American Made Precision Laser Death Rays” is the family business, now in its 52nd year.

Most memorable moment or biggest takeaway from VMI The VMI network is truly incredible and surpasses every other service out there because of all the different paths people choose after they graduate. It is amazing to see where classmates go and the things they discover.

What are you most excited about for the 25th Reunion? I have only been back briefly a few times, but I am interested to see Lexington and what has changed and what has stayed the same.

Kevin Consedine

Commander, Corpus Christi Army Depot

What are you doing now? I am currently serving as the Commander of Corpus Christi Army Depot, where I lead the Army's premier helicopter maintenance and sustainment organization. After 26 years in Army Aviation, I am fortunate to maintain my flight status and continue flying helicopters, which keeps me connected to the profession and Soldiers who first inspired me to serve.

As I prepare to retire from the Army next year, I am beginning to look toward a second career focused on the intersection of national security, public policy, and the industrial base. Outside of work, my wife, Meagan, and I stay busy raising our two teenagers. Much of our free time is spent traveling for our son's college soccer recruiting journey and our daughter's theater aspirations. I have also enjoyed writing on defense and industrial base issues for publications such as War on the Rocks and RealClearDefense.

Most memorable moment or biggest takeaway from VMI I do not have a single defining memory from VMI that stands above the rest. Instead, what has stayed with me is the foundation the Institute provided. VMI taught us that leadership begins with character and that integrity, accountability, and service to something greater than yourself are daily expectations.

Looking back 25 years later, I appreciate even more how unique that education was. The environment challenged us to develop resilience, humility, and the ability to lead under pressure. Those lessons have only become more relevant throughout my Army career, and I suspect they are even more valuable today than when we graduated.

What are you most excited about for the 25th Reunion? I am looking forward to reconnecting with classmates and hearing everyone's stories. We have all taken very different paths over the past 25 years, and I am excited to learn where life has taken everyone, professionally and personally. I am also looking forward to walking post again and remembering the friendships and shared experiences that continue to connect us.

Ring Resizing and Repairs

Send the ring insured to:

Balfour
Attn: Repair Dept.
7211 Circle S. Road
Austin, TX 78745
Balfour Repair Information

Include with the ring

  • Your name, repair instructions, return address, and daytime phone number.
  • A check or money order payable to Balfour for the $19.95 shipping, handling, and insurance charge.
  • An explicit note stating that the ring must not be remade when the ring has sentimental value and you do not authorize a remake.

Use a traceable shipping method such as USPS, UPS, or FedEx. Some repairs may require a remake.

Contact: collegerings@balfour.com or 1-800-BALFOUR, Monday through Friday, 8:30 a.m. to 5:00 p.m. Central Time.
